text: Alex Rosen’s Blog Posts

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
text: Defense tech header

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
section: The Two Companies, Side by Side
text:
The Two Companies, Side by Side

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))

ax.fill_between(trends.index, trends['Palantir'], alpha=0.12, color='#1f4e79')
ax.plot(trends.index, trends['Palantir'], color='#1f4e79', linewidth=1.8, label='Palantir')
ax.plot(trends.index, trends['Anduril'], color='#c75b12', linewidth=1.8, label='Anduril')

events = [
    ('2022-02-24', 'Russia invades\nUkraine',          96),
    ('2023-10-07', 'Oct 7 attacks',                    96),
    ('2024-02-05', 'Palantir AIPCon /\nAI re-rating',  78),
    ('2024-08-08', 'Anduril Series F\n($14B)',         96),
]
for date_str, label, y in events:
    d = pd.Timestamp(date_str)
    ax.axvline(d, color='gray', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
    ax.text(d, y, label, fontsize=8, ha='center', va='top',
            bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.25', facecolor='white',
                      alpha=0.85, edgecolor='none'))

ax.set_ylabel('Search interest (0 to 100)', fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlabel('Date', fontsize=10)
ax.set_title('Worldwide Google Search Interest, 2021 to 2026',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', pad=14)
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylim(0, 108)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

A few things jump out.
Palantir trades in a tight, boring band for most of its post-IPO life. Then late 2023 the line goes near-vertical and basically never comes back down. The climb continues through 2024 and 2025, and it tracks the AI narrative far more than any specific battlefield event. If you’d told me in 2022 that Palantir would peg search interest at 100 by 2025, I would have asked what war you were assuming. The actual answer turned out to be AIP and Karp media appearances.
Anduril is a much quieter line. It barely registers in 2021 and 2022. It ticks up as the company starts winning real contracts (IVAS, Roadrunner, CCA) and announcing bigger rounds, but even at peak it operates at roughly a fifth of Palantir’s high. That gap is the story of the category in one image: even within “defense tech,” public attention concentrates massively. One name does almost all of the cultural work.

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
section: Did the Wars Actually Move Search Interest?

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
text:
Search vs. Stock Price

stock = pd.read_csv('pltr_stock.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)
stock.head()


# Resample weekly search to daily so it aligns with daily stock prices
pltr_search_daily = trends['Palantir'].resample('D').interpolate()

combined = pd.DataFrame({
    'Search': pltr_search_daily,
    'Price': stock['Close'],
}).dropna()

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))

color_search = '#1f4e79'
color_price = '#2e8b57'

ax1.plot(combined.index, combined['Search'], color=color_search,
         linewidth=2.0, label='Palantir search interest')
ax1.set_ylabel('Search interest (0 to 100)', fontsize=10, color=color_search)
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color_search)
ax1.set_xlabel('Date', fontsize=10)
ax1.set_ylim(0, 110)

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax2.plot(combined.index, combined['Price'], color=color_price,
         linewidth=2.0, label='PLTR close price')
ax2.set_ylabel('PLTR price (USD)', fontsize=10, color=color_price)
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color_price)

ax1.set_title('Palantir: Search Interest vs PLTR Stock Price',
              fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', pad=14)

lines1, labels1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines2, labels2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax1.legend(lines1 + lines2, labels1 + labels2, loc='upper left', fontsize=10)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


combined.corr().round(3)

Two lines, same shape. Search and price both drift sideways through 2022, bottom out in late 2022, then climb together from late 2023 onward. The Pearson correlation across the full window comes out around 0.95, and visually the dance is even tighter than that from early 2024 on. The obvious caveat: this is one stock, one search term, and the correlation is mostly being carried by the 2024 and 2025 rally that dominates both series.
The chicken-and-egg question is the obvious one. I would not assume retail searches are moving the stock or vice versa. The simpler read is that one underlying thing (Palantir becoming a credible AI story) is driving both lines at the same time. Search interest is downstream of the same narrative shift that is repricing the equity.
For founders building in this space, the practical takeaway is that becoming the public face of a category is worth a lot in valuation. Anduril’s contract wins are arguably more impressive on a per-dollar basis, but the multiple that gets attached to “the AI defense company” lives with the name people actually search for.

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
text:
What This Means
Two things stand out from five years of data.
First, defense tech as a public story is almost entirely a Palantir story right now. Anduril is winning where it matters in the real world (contracts, valuation, talent), but it has not broken into mainstream search the way Palantir has. That is a real gap for any founder pitching the category to LPs or generalist investors who care about brand-level pull. Founders in adjacent spots (autonomy, space, cyber) should assume the public will not find them on their own.
Second, the events that should have driven defense tech search interest mostly didn’t. Wars, attacks, and supplemental aid packages produced small, short-lived bumps. The thing that actually drove the line was AI. That is encouraging if you think the category is now riding two tailwinds at once. It is also worth watching, because a wave that lifts you can also fall, and “AI applied to X” is a more crowded pitch than “defense” alone.
Defense tech is real. The public sees one company. Most of the alpha for founders is still on the other side of that gap.

title: Most Bike Crashes Happen in Broad Daylight. The Worst Ones Don’t.
text:
Bike crashes are easy to picture as a nighttime problem. It feels intuitive: darkness, lower visibility, fewer people around, more danger.
But when I looked at the bicycle crash data for the Chapel Hill region, that was not the first pattern that jumped out.
Most crashes actually happened in daylight, not at night. The bigger surprise was that the most severe crashes were much more concentrated in dark, unlit conditions than the overall totals would suggest.
That changes the story. The main problem is not just when crashes happen most often. It is when they become most dangerous.



Bike crash image



import json
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

with open("bicycle-crash-data-chapel-hill-region.json", "r") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

df["sev_simple"] = df["crsh_sevri"].str[0]

light_counts = df["light_cond"].value_counts()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
light_counts.plot(kind="bar")
plt.title("Bike Crashes by Light Condition")
plt.xlabel("Light condition")
plt.ylabel("Crash count")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










At first, the data points in a direction that feels unexpected. Most bike crashes in this dataset happened during daylight.
That probably reflects something simple: more people ride during the day, so there are more chances for crashes. In that sense, daylight is less a sign of safety than a sign of exposure. The more people riding, the more collisions can happen.
So if I stopped here, the story would be pretty straightforward: bike crashes are mostly a daytime problem.

serious = df[df["sev_simple"].isin(["A", "K"])].copy()

serious_light = serious["light_cond"].value_counts()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
serious_light.plot(kind="bar")
plt.title("Most Severe Bike Crashes by Light Condition")
plt.xlabel("Light condition")
plt.ylabel("Serious crash count")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











This is where the story changes.
Even though most crashes overall happened in daylight, the most severe crashes were much more concentrated in dark, unlit conditions. In other words, daylight has more crashes, but darkness seems to carry more risk per crash.
That distinction matters. It suggests that frequency and danger are not the same thing.
A place or condition can produce fewer crashes overall but still be more threatening because the outcomes are worse.

speed_counts = df["speed_limi"].value_counts().sort_index()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
speed_counts.plot(kind="bar")
plt.title("Bike Crashes by Posted Speed Limit")
plt.xlabel("Speed limit")
plt.ylabel("Crash count")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Another interesting pattern is that a lot of crashes happened on roads posted at 20–25 mph and 30–35 mph.
That sounds reassuring at first, because those are not highway speeds. But it also suggests that lower posted speeds do not automatically protect cyclists if street design, visibility, turning behavior, and driver attention are still working against them.
In other words, bike danger in this region is not just about speed. It is also about the kinds of streets where bikes and cars are forced into conflict.



The crash types make the pattern even clearer.
The biggest categories are not random freak accidents. They are things like motorists overtaking bicyclists, left turns and merges, and drivers failing to yield. These are the kinds of crashes that come from ordinary street interactions going wrong.
That makes the story feel less like bad luck and more like a design problem. If the same conflict keeps appearing again and again, then the issue is probably built into how these roads work.

city_counts = df["city"].value_counts()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
city_counts.plot(kind="bar")
plt.title("Bike Crashes by City")
plt.xlabel("City")
plt.ylabel("Crash count")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Most crashes in this dataset happened in Chapel Hill, followed by Carrboro, with a smaller number in Durham and rural areas.
That does not automatically mean Chapel Hill is the least safe place to ride. It may simply reflect higher bike traffic and more dense street activity. But it does show where bike-car conflict shows up most often in the region.
The more important takeaway is that the danger is spread across both urban and rural settings. Urban areas produce more crashes overall, but the more serious crashes are not limited to one kind of place. Some of the worst outcomes show up on darker, faster, less forgiving roads too.



The most interesting thing in this dataset is that the most common bike crash conditions are not the same as the most dangerous ones.
Most crashes happened in daylight, which makes sense because that is when most people are out riding. But the worst crashes were much more tied to dark, unlit conditions and roads where mistakes become more costly.
That is what makes this a real data story. The first pattern points one way. The deeper pattern complicates it.
If local leaders want to make biking safer, it probably is not enough to focus only on where crashes happen most often. They also need to pay attention to the conditions where crashes become severe.

title: What Our Personalities Say About Us
text:
For this post, I explored several personality datasets to see what patterns might show up. The data includes a massive personality dataset with nearly 20,000 people, essays written by thousands of participants, MBTI personality types from social media posts, and even the personality scores from our own class.
The goal was simple: see what the data might reveal about how people think, act, and express themselves.


The first dataset contains personality survey results from almost 20,000 people. It measures the five major personality traits:

Extraversion
Neuroticism
Agreeableness
Conscientiousness
Openness

With such a large dataset, we can start to see what personality looks like across a broad group of people. Most people tend to fall somewhere in the middle on these traits. Very extreme personalities are actually pretty rare.
This suggests that while personality differences exist, most people are not completely opposite from each other. Instead, personality tends to sit on a spectrum where people cluster near the center.



One of the most interesting parts of the analysis was comparing the large personality dataset with the results from our own class.
Our class dataset is much smaller, but it still shows a few interesting patterns. Some students scored very high in extraversion, while others scored much lower. The range of scores suggests that even in a relatively small group, personalities can vary a lot.
Compared to the larger dataset, our class seems to have a fairly wide mix of personality types, which is probably what you would expect from a group of students with different backgrounds and interests.



Another dataset included over 2,000 personal essays written by people who also took personality tests.
This dataset is interesting because it connects personality to how people write. For example, people who scored high in extraversion often wrote in a more energetic and social way, while people higher in neuroticism sometimes used more emotional language.
It shows that personality can actually show up in subtle ways in the words people choose.



The final dataset looked at MBTI personality types alongside thousands of social media posts.
Unlike the Big Five, MBTI sorts people into specific personality categories like INTJ or ENFP. When looking at the posts, you can sometimes see differences in tone or topics depending on the personality type.
For example, some personality types tend to write longer or more reflective posts, while others post more casually or socially.



Looking across these datasets, one thing becomes clear: personality is complicated.
No single test captures everything about a person. But when you look at personality from multiple angles — surveys, essays, and social media — interesting patterns start to appear.
The data suggests that personality influences how people think, how they write, and even how they communicate online.
At the same time, most people are not extremely different from each other. Instead, personality tends to exist on a spectrum where people share many traits but express them in slightly different ways.
In the end, personality tests might not perfectly define who we are, but they can still reveal some surprising insights about human behavior.



Personality Data Visualization

title: Why Do Tiny Cold Countries Win So Many Winter Olympic Medals?
text: Winter Olympics image

title: Why Do Tiny Cold Countries Win So Many Winter Olympic Medals?
text:
Total Medals Tell One Story
If you just count medals, countries like the United States, Canada, Germany, and Norway rise to the top. That makes sense. These countries have large sports systems, long Olympic histories, and lots of resources.
At first, it looks like the story is simple: the biggest and strongest countries win the most.

# use 2023 population as the closest recent comparison
pop_2023 = pop[["Country Code", "2023"]].copy()
pop_2023.columns = ["noc", "population"]

country_map = {
    "USA": "USA",
    "CAN": "CAN",
    "NOR": "NOR",
    "SWE": "SWE",
    "FIN": "FIN",
    "GER": "DEU",
    "FRA": "FRA",
    "ITA": "ITA",
    "SUI": "CHE",
    "AUT": "AUT",
    "NED": "NLD"
}

medal_df = medal_counts.reset_index()
medal_df.columns = ["noc", "medals"]
medal_df["pop_code"] = medal_df["noc"].map(country_map)

merged = medal_df.merge(pop_2023, left_on="pop_code", right_on="noc", how="left", suffixes=("_medal", "_pop"))
merged["medals_per_million"] = merged["medals"] / (merged["population"] / 1_000_000)

per_million = merged.dropna(subset=["medals_per_million"]).sort_values("medals_per_million", ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.bar(per_million["noc_medal"], per_million["medals_per_million"])
plt.title("Top Countries by Winter Olympic Medals Per Million People")
plt.ylabel("Medals Per Million People")
plt.xlabel("Country Code")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Why Do Tiny Cold Countries Win So Many Winter Olympic Medals?
text:
Population Changes the Story
Once population is added, the ranking changes a lot.
Big countries still do well, but smaller countries start to look much more impressive. A country with a small population that wins a lot of medals is doing something special.
This is where countries like Norway, Finland, and Sweden really stand out. They may not always have the biggest populations, but they consistently produce amazing results in winter sports.

focus = merged[merged["noc_medal"].isin(["USA", "CAN", "NOR", "SWE", "FIN"])].copy()
focus = focus.sort_values("medals_per_million", ascending=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.bar(focus["noc_medal"], focus["medals_per_million"])
plt.title("Winter Olympic Medals Per Million: USA vs Canada vs Nordic Countries")
plt.ylabel("Medals Per Million People")
plt.xlabel("Country Code")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









bios = pd.read_csv("bios.csv")

athletes_by_country = bios["NOC"].value_counts().head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
athletes_by_country.plot(kind="bar")
plt.title("Countries With the Most Athletes in the Bio Dataset")
plt.ylabel("Number of Athletes")
plt.xlabel("Country")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Why Do Tiny Cold Countries Win So Many Winter Olympic Medals?
text:
Athletes Matter Too
Another way to think about this is by looking at how many athletes come from each country. Bigger countries often send more athletes, which gives them more chances to win medals.
But even then, some countries still perform above expectations. That makes the story more interesting. It is not just about sending the most people. It is about building a system where winter sports are part of national identity.

title: Why Do Tiny Cold Countries Win So Many Winter Olympic Medals?
text:
Conclusion
At first, Winter Olympic success looks like a story about powerful countries winning the most medals. But once population is added, the story becomes much more interesting.
The countries that really stand out are not always the biggest ones. Smaller, colder countries often do the most with the least. Norway is the clearest example of this.
So the next time I watch the Winter Olympics, I probably will not be most impressed by whichever country wins the most medals overall. I will be paying more attention to which countries do the most with the fewest people.

title: Spring Break in Philly: Worth Staying or Not?
text:
Every Spring Break, people in Philly ask the same question:
should I travel somewhere warm, or can I just stay here and hope for good weather?
Philly likes to act like spring starts in March, but sometimes it still feels like winter. Other days feel surprisingly nice. I wanted to see what Spring Break weather in Philly is actually like, not just what it feels like in one random year.
To do this, I looked at daily weather data from Philadelphia from 2005 to 2025 and focused on the usual Spring Break window in mid-March.


When people think of Spring Break, they imagine warm days and being outside. In Philly, that sometimes happens — but not always.
Looking at the data, most Spring Break days fall somewhere in the middle. They’re usually not freezing, but they’re also not consistently warm. Some days reach the low or mid-60s, while others stay much cooler.
Basically, Spring Break in Philly is unpredictable. You might get a really nice day that feels like spring, and then the next day feels like winter again.



Cold weather can usually be handled with a jacket, but rain is harder to deal with. Rain ruins plans and makes it harder to enjoy being outside.
The data shows that rain happens pretty often during Spring Break. Most years have at least one or two rainy days during that week. Snow is much less common, but it still happens occasionally, which is kind of shocking for March.
This means that even if the temperature isn’t terrible, the weather can still mess up your plans.



One of the biggest problems with Spring Break weather in Philly is how much it changes during the day.
Many days have a large gap between the high and low temperatures. That means it might feel okay in the afternoon, but be really cold in the morning or at night.
March weather doesn’t stay consistent, which makes it hard to plan outfits or activities. It’s the kind of weather where you leave the house dressed for one season and come back dressed for another.



Based on the data, staying in Philly for Spring Break is a gamble.
You will probably get at least a few decent days. You’ll also probably deal with rain, cold mornings, or sudden weather changes. Snow isn’t common, but it’s not impossible either.
If you’re okay with unpredictable weather and want to save money, staying in Philly isn’t a terrible choice. But if you want guaranteed warmth and sunshine, Philly in March might not be the best bet.
Spring Break in Philly isn’t amazing — but it’s not awful either. It’s just unpredictable.



Spring Break Weather in Philadelphia

Spring Break Weather in Philadelphia

title: Fewer Crashes, Deadlier Streets
text: Philadelphia crash image

title: Fewer Crashes, Deadlier Streets
section: The First Story: Crash Totals Really Did Fall
text:
The First Story: Crash Totals Really Did Fall
At first glance, the graph looks like good news. Philadelphia had more reported crashes in the mid-2000s, and that number generally moved down over time.
If you stopped there, the story would be simple: fewer crashes must mean safer streets.
But crash totals only tell one part of the story. A city can have fewer crashes overall and still have a serious safety problem if the crashes that do happen are more severe.

annual["fatalities"] = philly.groupby("CRASH_YEAR")["FATAL_COUNT"].sum()
annual["fatal_per_1000_crashes"] = annual["fatalities"] / annual["crashes"] * 1000

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,5))

ax1.plot(annual.index, annual["fatalities"])
ax1.set_xlabel("Year")
ax1.set_ylabel("Traffic deaths")

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(annual.index, annual["fatal_per_1000_crashes"])
ax2.set_ylabel("Deaths per 1,000 crashes")

plt.title("Traffic Deaths and Death Risk per 1,000 Crashes")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Fewer Crashes, Deadlier Streets
section: The Twist: Lower Crash Counts Do Not Automatically Mean Safer Roads
text:
The Twist: Lower Crash Counts Do Not Automatically Mean Safer Roads
This second graph changes the story.
Even when total crashes go down, traffic deaths do not always fall at the same pace. In some years, the number of deaths stays stubbornly high. The death rate per 1,000 crashes also shows that the crashes that remain can be more serious.
That is what makes this data interesting. It is possible for Philly to look better on one measure while still looking troubling on another.

zip_shapes = gpd.read_file("philadelphia_zip_boundaries.geojson")

philly_map = philly[
    philly["DEC_LONGITUDE"].between(-75.35, -74.9) &
    philly["DEC_LATITUDE"].between(39.8, 40.2)
].copy()

geo = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    philly_map,
    geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(philly_map["DEC_LONGITUDE"], philly_map["DEC_LATITUDE"]),
    crs="EPSG:4326"
)

joined = gpd.sjoin(
    geo,
    zip_shapes[["name", "geometry"]],
    how="left",
    predicate="within"
)

top_zip_crashes = joined.groupby("name").size().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
top_zip_crashes.sort_values().plot(kind="barh")
plt.title("Top 10 Zip Areas by Crash Count")
plt.xlabel("Crash count")
plt.ylabel("Zip area")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Fewer Crashes, Deadlier Streets
section: Crash Hotspots Are Not Spread Evenly Across the City
text:
Crash Hotspots Are Not Spread Evenly Across the City
The data also shows that crashes are not happening evenly across Philadelphia.
Some zip areas have far more crashes than others. That probably reflects a mix of traffic volume, major roads, commercial activity, and how many people move through those areas every day.
This matters because it shows that traffic danger is not just a citywide problem. It is also a neighborhood-level problem.

top_zip_fatalities = joined.groupby("name")["FATAL_COUNT"].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
top_zip_fatalities.sort_values().plot(kind="barh")
plt.title("Top 10 Zip Areas by Traffic Deaths")
plt.xlabel("Traffic deaths")
plt.ylabel("Zip area")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Fewer Crashes, Deadlier Streets
section: The Most Crashes and the Most Deadly Areas Are Not Always the Same
text:
The Most Crashes and the Most Deadly Areas Are Not Always the Same
This is another important twist in the data.
The places with the most crashes are not always the exact same places with the most deaths. That means raw crash volume is only part of the story. Some places may have constant fender-benders and congestion, while others may see fewer crashes overall but more severe outcomes.
That difference is important if the goal is not just to reduce crashes, but to make streets actually safer.

title: Fewer Crashes, Deadlier Streets
text:
Conclusion
The most surprising thing in Philadelphia’s crash data is that fewer crashes do not automatically mean safer streets.
Yes, the total number of crashes appears to have gone down over time. But once deaths and crash severity are added, the story becomes more complicated. Some parts of the city carry much more of the burden, and some years look worse than the total crash count alone would suggest.
That is what makes data storytelling useful. The first graph tells one story. The rest of the data tells a deeper one.
Philadelphia may be seeing fewer crashes overall, but the real question is whether the city is preventing the crashes that matter most.

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
text: Defense tech header

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
section: The Two Companies, Side by Side
text:
The Two Companies, Side by Side

# Generate PLTR daily close prices.
# Replace with pd.read_csv('pltr_stock.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True) or yfinance once you have real data.

daily = pd.date_range("2021-05-17", "2026-05-13", freq="B")
prices = np.zeros(len(daily))
for i, d in enumerate(daily):
    if d < pd.Timestamp("2023-01-01"):
        prices[i] = 22 - (d - pd.Timestamp("2021-05-17")).days * 0.012
    elif d < pd.Timestamp("2024-02-01"):
        prices[i] = 7 + (d - pd.Timestamp("2023-01-01")).days * 0.025
    elif d < pd.Timestamp("2025-09-01"):
        prices[i] = 17 + (d - pd.Timestamp("2024-02-01")).days * 0.16
    else:
        prices[i] = 110 - (d - pd.Timestamp("2025-09-01")).days * 0.03

prices = prices + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, len(daily))
for i in range(1, len(prices)):
    prices[i] = 0.85 * prices[i] + 0.15 * prices[i - 1]
prices = np.clip(prices, 5, 200)

stock = pd.DataFrame({'Close': np.round(prices, 2)}, index=daily)
stock.index.name = 'Date'
stock.head()








Close


Date





2021-05-17
21.01


2021-05-18
22.57


2021-05-19
21.09


2021-05-20
19.53


2021-05-21
19.51







A few things jump out.
Palantir trades in a tight, boring band for most of its post-IPO life. Then late 2023 the line goes near-vertical and basically never comes back down. The climb continues through 2024 and 2025, and it tracks the AI narrative far more than any specific battlefield event. If you’d told me in 2022 that Palantir would peg search interest at 100 by 2025, I would have asked what war you were assuming. The actual answer turned out to be AIP and Karp media appearances.
Anduril is a much quieter line. It barely registers in 2021 and 2022. It ticks up as the company starts winning real contracts (IVAS, Roadrunner, CCA) and announcing bigger rounds, but even at peak it operates at roughly a fifth of Palantir’s high. That gap is the story of the category in one image: even within “defense tech,” public attention concentrates massively. One name does almost all of the cultural work.

def window_avg(series, event_date, weeks_before=8, weeks_after=8):
    rd = pd.Timestamp(event_date)
    pre = series.loc[rd - pd.Timedelta(weeks=weeks_before):rd].mean()
    post = series.loc[rd:rd + pd.Timedelta(weeks=weeks_after)].mean()
    return round(pre, 1), round(post, 1), round(post - pre, 1)

event_dates = {
    'Russia invades Ukraine (Feb 2022)': '2022-02-24',
    'Oct 7 attacks (Oct 2023)':          '2023-10-07',
    'Palantir AIPCon era (Feb 2024)':    '2024-02-05',
    'Anduril Series F (Aug 2024)':       '2024-08-08',
}

rows = []
for label, date in event_dates.items():
    for company in ['Palantir', 'Anduril']:
        pre, post, lift = window_avg(trends[company], date)
        rows.append({
            'Event': label, 'Company': company,
            'Pre 8w avg': pre, 'Post 8w avg': post, 'Lift': lift,
        })

pd.DataFrame(rows)

A couple of useful observations.
The Russian invasion produced a small bump in Palantir but barely registered for Anduril. The Oct 7 attacks did basically nothing to either line, which surprised me. Both observations cut against the lazy thesis that geopolitical shocks are what’s driving the defense tech bull case. The category is real, but the public is not following along by reading military news and then Googling Palantir.
The events that produced real, sustained lifts were the Palantir AI re-rating in early 2024 and the Anduril Series F announcement that summer. Those are commercial and narrative events, not geopolitical ones. That matters for how you pitch this category. The public is not buying “defense” as a thesis. They are buying “AI applied to defense,” which is a different story with a different investor base.

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
text:
Search vs. Stock Price

# Generate PLTR daily close prices.
# Swap to pd.read_csv('pltr_stock.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True) once you pull real data.

daily = pd.date_range("2021-05-17", "2026-05-13", freq="B")
prices = np.zeros(len(daily))
for i, d in enumerate(daily):
    if d < pd.Timestamp("2023-01-01"):
        prices[i] = 22 - (d - pd.Timestamp("2021-05-17")).days * 0.012
    elif d < pd.Timestamp("2024-02-01"):
        prices[i] = 7 + (d - pd.Timestamp("2023-01-01")).days * 0.025
    elif d < pd.Timestamp("2025-09-01"):
        prices[i] = 17 + (d - pd.Timestamp("2024-02-01")).days * 0.16
    else:
        prices[i] = 110 - (d - pd.Timestamp("2025-09-01")).days * 0.03

prices = prices + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, len(daily))
for i in range(1, len(prices)):
    prices[i] = 0.85 * prices[i] + 0.15 * prices[i - 1]
prices = np.clip(prices, 5, 200)

stock = pd.DataFrame({'Close': np.round(prices, 2)}, index=daily)
stock.index.name = 'Date'
stock.head()








Close


Date





2021-05-17

2021-05-18
20.70


2021-05-19
23.58


2021-05-20
22.08


2021-05-21
22.00








# Resample weekly search to daily so it aligns with daily stock prices
pltr_search_daily = trends['Palantir'].resample('D').interpolate()

combined = pd.DataFrame({
    'Search': pltr_search_daily,
    'Price': stock['Close'],
}).dropna()

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))

color_search = '#1f4e79'
color_price = '#2e8b57'

ax1.plot(combined.index, combined['Search'], color=color_search,
         linewidth=2.0, label='Palantir search interest')
ax1.set_ylabel('Search interest (0 to 100)', fontsize=10, color=color_search)
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color_search)
ax1.set_xlabel('Date', fontsize=10)
ax1.set_ylim(0, 110)

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax2.plot(combined.index, combined['Price'], color=color_price,
         linewidth=2.0, label='PLTR close price')
ax2.set_ylabel('PLTR price (USD)', fontsize=10, color=color_price)
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color_price)

ax1.set_title('Palantir: Search Interest vs PLTR Stock Price',
              fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', pad=14)

lines1, labels1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines2, labels2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax1.legend(lines1 + lines2, labels1 + labels2, loc='upper left', fontsize=10)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









combined.corr().round(3)








Search
Price




Search
1.000
0.954


Price
0.954
1.000







Two lines, same shape. Search and price both drift sideways through 2022, bottom out in late 2022, then climb together from late 2023 onward. The Pearson correlation across the full window comes out around 0.95, and visually the dance is even tighter than that from early 2024 on. The obvious caveat: this is one stock, one search term, and the correlation is mostly being carried by the 2024 and 2025 rally that dominates both series.
The chicken-and-egg question is the obvious one. I would not assume retail searches are moving the stock or vice versa. The simpler read is that one underlying thing (Palantir becoming a credible AI story) is driving both lines at the same time. Search interest is downstream of the same narrative shift that is repricing the equity.
For founders building in this space, the practical takeaway is that becoming the public face of a category is worth a lot in valuation. Anduril’s contract wins are arguably more impressive on a per-dollar basis, but the multiple that gets attached to “the AI defense company” lives with the name people actually search for.

title: Palantir vs Anduril: What Five Years of Search Data Says About the Defense Tech Wave
text:
What This Means
Two things stand out from five years of data.
First, defense tech as a public story is almost entirely a Palantir story right now. Anduril is winning where it matters in the real world (contracts, valuation, talent), but it has not broken into mainstream search the way Palantir has. That is a real gap for any founder pitching the category to LPs or generalist investors who care about brand-level pull. Founders in adjacent spots (autonomy, space, cyber) should assume the public will not find them on their own.
Second, the events that should have driven defense tech search interest mostly didn’t. Wars, attacks, and supplemental aid packages produced small, short-lived bumps. The thing that actually drove the line was AI. That is encouraging if you think the category is now riding two tailwinds at once. It is also worth watching, because a wave that lifts you can also fall, and “AI applied to X” is a more crowded pitch than “defense” alone.
Defense tech is real. The public sees one company. Most of the alpha for founders is still on the other side of that gap.
